chunks.text: My grandfather was a man who hardly spoke.
He seldom talked about himself, and rarely showed his emotions outside.
When I was young, that confused me.
What was he thinking?
Did he dislike me?
But as the years passed, I came to interpret him.
His silence was not coldness, but a quiet love.
The generation that lived through the war tends to avoid talking about it.
Remembering itself calls pain.
They touch the past only briefly.
After my grandfather passed away, I found notebooks he had been writing.
In them, his detailed memories of the battlefield, and his intimate thoughts toward family
were recorded in small handwriting.
He had truly loved us.
It was simply uncomfortable for him to put it into words.
For him, love was shown through action.
Tilling the field with me on his day off
secretly sending a small gift on his grandchild's birthday.
Inside my grandfather's silence, I grasped true kindness.
Words had not been useless — rather, there was a love that went beyond words.
On my late grandfather's birthday, I go to his grave to offer flowers.
I briefly join my hands and quietly speak to him for a short time.
My grandfather, even now, in an invisible form, lives within my heart.
